item.title: A Nonisolated Switched-Capacitor-Based Bidirectional DC–DC Converter With Low Input Current Ripple and Common Ground for EV Application
item.description: In electric vehicle applications, bidirectional DC–DC converters with low input current ripple are ideal due to the sensitivity and charging characteristics of energy storage systems such as batteries and super-capacitors. This article introduces a nonisolated bidirectional converter that hybridly integrates a coupled-inductor and switched-inductor-capacitor cell at the input with a traditional bidirectional DC–DC topology, while quasi-hybridly incorporating a switched-capacitor cell at the output. The proposed converter achieves high voltage gain, minimal input current ripple, low semiconductor voltage stress, and high efficiency. It further offers key features such as low component count, shared common ground between the input and output to mitigate electromagnetic interference, and soft-switching capability in both buck and boost modes, which reduce switching losses, cooling demands, system volume, and cost, making it well-suited for EV applications. Steady-state analysis and design of the proposed converter are provided, with its advantages assessed through a comparison with other similar structures. Furthermore, its Controllability is enhanced through an improved pole-placement control strategy. Finally, the theoretical results are verified by experimental results obtained from a 300-W laboratory prototype operating at 50 kHz and a 400-V output. Theoretical maximum efficiencies at 150 W are 97.72% in boost mode and 96.88% in buck mode.

item.title: Variable Reference Voltage PWM for Five-Level Stacked Multicell Converter With Capacitor Voltage Balancing and Reduced Common-Mode Voltage
item.description: Carrier-based modulation strategies with natural capacitor voltage balancing characteristics are widely employed in five-level stacked multicell (SM) converters, owing to their straightforward implementation and reduced capacitor requirements. However, active voltage balancing of DC-link capacitors usually requires zero-sequence voltage (ZSV) or harmonic voltage injection, which introduces significant common-mode voltage (CMV) and degrades system performance. To overcome this limitation, this article proposes a variable reference voltage pulsewidth modulation (PWM) (VRV-PWM) strategy. The proposed method leverages a novel neutral-point (NP) current expression, derived from the SM converter’s switching states, to regulate the NP current via duty cycle adjustment, thereby operating without ZSV or harmonic injection. Consequently, this approach maintains an average phase voltage free of low-frequency components while achieving zero average CMV, which significantly suppresses the instantaneous CMV and enhances the output phase voltage performance. An optimal NP current regulation scheme is further derived to achieve independent and decoupled voltage control across all capacitors. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed strategy complements traditional methods in NP voltage balancing, effectively overcoming their drawback in suppressing low-frequency voltage fluctuations under low power factor conditions. Experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed VRV-PWM, demonstrating its superior performance in CMV reduction and output phase voltage harmonic quality.

item.title: Charge Cycle Efficiency and Grid Power Quality Enhancement of an Off-Board Battery Charger Using Model Predictive Duty Cycle Control
item.description: The conventional finite control set model predictive control applied to battery chargers exhibits a variable switching frequency operation. Subsequently, it becomes difficult to select appropriate semiconductor devices, design and tune passive components, and voltage/current sensing filters due to nonuniform thermal stress on semiconductor devices and unclean/noisy frequency spectrum, especially in grid-connected systems. To address the aforementioned concerns, this article introduces a model predictive duty cycle control (MPDCC) strategy for a two-stage (AC–DC and DC–DC) off-board battery charger with fixed switching frequency operation. This method generates duty cycles using optimal sector determination, voltage vector selection, and cost function minimization. In addition, a new time-weighted average efficiency (TWAE) metric is introduced to quantify charger performance under constant current–constant voltage and constant power–constant voltage charging profiles. To validate the efficacy of the proposed MPDCC method, a two-stage off-board charger system is developed and experimental studies are conducted on a 3-kW laboratory charger prototype with a dSPACE MicroLabBox-DS1202 control platform to charge the 2.4-kWh, 400 V battery pack under different charging modes. Finally, the charger’s performance using the proposed MPDCC approach is compared with existing control methods in terms of grid power quality, battery voltage/current ripples, average switching frequency, peak efficiency, and the TWAE efficiency to show its superiority.

item.title: Grid Integrated Single-Stage Current-Fed Three-Port Converter for High and Low Voltage Battery Charging in Electric Vehicles
item.description: This article proposes a new three-port integrated onboard charger topology for electric vehicles, enabling simultaneous charging to both high-voltage (HV) and low-voltage (LV) batteries. The proposed topology combines a single-phase single-stage current-fed onboard charger with a low-voltage auxiliary power module through a three-winding high-frequency transformer, eliminating the conventional dedicated power factor correction stage. For simultaneous power transfer within this integrated topology, the dependency of the LV-side duty cycle variation on the sinusoidal modulation of the grid and HV side converters is established. Based on this analysis, an adaptive sinusoidal pulse width modulation strategy is proposed to dynamically accommodate these duty variations, thereby ensuring simultaneous power distribution to the loads. In addition, steady-state analysis and various operational modes of the proposed topology across switching intervals over the grid cycle are also discussed thoroughly. A 2.2 kW experimental prototype is developed to validate the proposed integrated three-port converter operation under varying load conditions. Experimental results demonstrate a power factor above 0.99 over the entire operating range and a peak efficiency of 95.04$\%$ during simultaneous power transfer to both batteries.

item.title: A Bus-Voltage-Adaptive Dual-Mode Stacked Half-Bridge LCLC Converter for Electric Vehicles Energy Router Applications
item.description: High-voltage electric vehicle (EV) battery platforms challenge fast charging compatibility. High-voltage EV battery platforms face challenges in fast-charging compatibility. A dual-battery pack scheme utilizing a double-pole double-throw (DPDT) switch improves charging compatibility, but results in an excessively wide bus voltage. Conventional auxiliary power modules (APMs) become unsuitable under such conditions. This article therefore proposes an adaptive dual-mode buck/boost converter for HV/LV platforms. First, by altering its connection configuration, the proposed converter is better suited for dual-battery-pack systems utilizing DPDT switches. It can accommodate a wide bus voltage range from 250 to 1000 V (or even higher), while the maximum voltage stress on its mosfet is only 500 V. Second, in boost mode at both platforms, the converter is fundamentally reconfigured into an LLC converter with a parallel LC branch on the primary side (LCLC converter). The magnetizing inductance of this converter increases with the switching frequency, enabling the required voltage gain while simultaneously suppressing circulating current losses. Third, in buck mode at both platforms, it operates as an LLC converter with a larger resonant inductance and higher quality factor, thereby enhancing step-down capability within a narrower frequency variation range. Finally, A 2.4 kW prototype with 96$\%$ peak efficiency (250–1000 V input, 48 V output) validates the design performance.

item.title: Analytical Solution-Based Active Discharge Algorithm for IPMSM Considering Safety Constraints
item.description: An analytical solution-based active discharge method is developed for IPMSM to minimize regenerative torque. The method is designed to reduce the risk of electrical shock and is typically employed when switching off the vehicle. However, in fault conditions, the connection between the high-voltage battery and the motor control unit is disconnected, and active discharging is requested. During active discharging, the motor can be rotated due to the vehicle's inertia. To execute the active discharge method, $dq$-axis currents of the motor should be utilized, which may result in unintended regenerative torque. To reduce unintended regenerative torque, the DC-link capacitor current model is derived through a power flow analysis of the motor drive system. The behavior of the capacitor current is examined in the $dq$-axis current plane, revealing its nonlinear dependence on motor speed and DC-link voltage. To mitigate this nonlinearity and achieve minimum regenerative torque during active discharging, a nonlinear optimization problem is formulated to find $dq$-axis currents under control and safety constraints. The feasible solutions are classified into three cases based on these constraints, and quartic equations are derived and analytically solved using the Ferrari's method. Simulation and experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach in reducing regenerative torque while ensuring compliant voltage discharge.

item.title: Robust Deadbeat Predictive Current Control for PMSM Harmonic Torque Suppression Based on Multiloop Quasi-Resonant Observer
item.description: Although permanent magnet synchronous motors offer high efficiency and power density, their performance is often compromised by inherent harmonic currents and torque pulsations, limiting applicability in high-precision motion control. While conventional deadbeat predictive current control (DPCC) improves dynamic response and control performance, it fails to mitigate harmonic-induced vibration due to its limited observation capability for high-frequency disturbances. To address these challenges, this article proposes a harmonic-suppression DPCC with a decoupled compensation structure. First, a parameter-robust extended state observer is designed to actively estimate and compensate for low-frequency disturbances caused by parameter variations. Second, parallel multiloop quasi-resonant controllers are embedded in both the current and speed loops to provide high gain at targeted harmonic frequencies, enabling precise cancellation of periodic torque harmonics. Electromechanical simulations and experiments validate the proposed approach, demonstrating significant harmonic suppression and robust performance. The results confirm its potential for high-precision, low-vibration motion control applications.

item.title: Energy Management Strategy for Fuel Cell Hybrid Electric Vehicle Based on Driving Pattern Recognition With Fuzzy C-Means Clustering
item.description: Fuel cell vehicles have broad prospects in the field of transportation electrification, while their operating characteristics limit their application in complex working conditions. As a result, fuel cell vehicles are generally equipped with Li-ion batteries or ultra-capacitors as auxiliary power sources. An energy management strategy (EMS) is essential to maintain the efficient, stable, and reliable operation of fuel cell hybrid electric vehicles. In this work, a driving pattern recognition (DPR) method is introduced to improve the adaptability of the EMS. First, fuzzy C-means clustering is applied to classify different driving patterns, facilitating the construction of representative driving cycles. Under each driving cycle, the parameters of fuzzy logic controllers are optimized using a genetic algorithm. Finally, the corresponding fuzzy logic controller is invoked based on the DPR results for power allocation in this hybrid power electronic system. The proposed method reduces the overall cost by at least 9.94% compared with a fuzzy logic control strategy, while remaining within 1.83% of the dynamic programming strategy, demonstrating its near-optimal performance with real-time feasibility.

item.title: A Three-Phase Underwater Wireless Power Transfer System With Robust Anti-Misalignment and Parameter Drift Tolerance for AUV Applications
item.description: Due to ocean current fluctuations, misalignment is prone to occur between autonomous underwater vehicles and charging docks. This induces drastic variations in the mutual inductance of underwater wireless power transfer (UWPT) systems, thereby leading to a significant decline in the power transfer capacity and efficiency of the system. Furthermore, parameter drift can significantly impact the implementation and operation range of soft-switching. To overcome these issues, this article proposes a three-phase UWPT system employing a segmented fan-shaped coupler. This system demonstrates robust antimisalignment performance under axial and angular misalignment conditions. Moreover, by integrating an active auxiliary network, the system enables zero-voltage switching over the full load range. The implementation of this soft-switching approach is not susceptible to the fluctuations of compensation parameter values and has a strong ability to resist such fluctuations. The operating principle of the proposed UWPT system is first elaborated. Subsequently, considering the dynamic marine environment, comprehensive rules for achieving full-range soft-switching are derived. The design methodology for the segmented fan-shaped coupler is then presented. Finally, a 1-kW experimental prototype is built to validate the feasibility and performance of the proposed scheme .

item.title: Deep Q-Network-Based Power Management for Emission-Aware Optimization in Port Ship Microgrid Systems
item.description: The control and optimization of shipboard microgrid operations in port environments pose increasing challenges due to limited shore power capacity and stringent emissions regulations. This article presents a power electronics-oriented control strategy that leverages a Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework to achieve emission-aware energy management in ship auxiliary systems. The proposed method enables bidirectional power coordination between shore power infrastructure and the ship’s onboard microgrid, targeting minimum emission point tracking. By integrating deep reinforcement learning with power system control, the DQN approach dynamically adapts to varying load profiles and ensures optimal engine dispatch, reducing reliance on high-emission operation modes. Additionally, energy storage units are incorporated to enhance system flexibility, with real-time control of battery state of charge and adaptive response to specific fuel consumption variations. Various scenarios are conducted, including robustness tests and analysis under varying load profiles, grid availability, stochastic uncertainties, and realistic berthing load conditions, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed DQN achieves a higher reduction in emissions by 6.69% and faster convergence to the optimal operational state compared to advanced reinforcement learning approaches (deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG), twin delayed DDPG, soft actor–critic, and proximal policy optimization) and traditional rule-based methods. These results highlight the potential of intelligent power electronics control for sustainable and high-efficiency maritime applications.

item.title: Multiport Arm Shared DC–DC Converter With Decoupling Control for Multivoltage DC Grid
item.description: Multiport dc–dc converters are the core exchange hubs of dc grids, especially in new energy partition access and complex ring network scenarios. This article proposes a multiport arm shared dc–dc (ASdc) converter with flexible control of voltage and current at each port. For the multiport architecture, a decoupling control strategy using node voltage control instead of the energy balance control between arms and a split-arm start-up method based on MMC ac/dc side active charging are proposed. The proposed control enhances the stability and robustness of the ASdc converters under complex power variations at multiple ports. And the proposed start-up method reduces the difficulty of start-up implementation and has the advantages of low start-up stress and strong independence of each port. A prototype of a downscaled ASdc converter with bipolar dual-LV-port structure, rated at 1.5 kW, ±240 V/±75 V, is built. The experimental results agree with the theoretical analyses and show that the proposed dc–dc converter with its control strategy and start-up method is very effective.

item.title: Trapezoidal Wave Operation of T-Type DC/DC Converter for High-Power Applications
item.description: All-DC collection and transmission is a promising solution for integration of large-scale renewable energy in desert and deep-sea areas. High-power DC/DC converter is the key equipment in this all-DC system. However, current T-type DC/DC converter (T-DC) based on modular multilevel technology faces high current stress and limited power capacity. In this article, trapezoidal wave operation of T-DC is proposed to reduce device current stress and thus increase power capacity. Waveform constraints are clarified, and both symmetrical trapezoidal wave (STW) and asymmetrical trapezoidal wave (ATW) operation are proposed. Compared with the traditional sinusoidal wave operation, the proposed STW and ATW operation both offer higher power capacity, reduced capacitor energy storage, and improved efficiency in a wide voltage ratio range. The advantages of ATW become more pronounced at higher voltage ratios. In addition, to mitigate significant fundamental-frequency current ripple on the DC side caused by unbalanced T-DC losses between phases, an arm energy balance method based on internal AC and DC voltage regulation is proposed, eliminating the ripple without the need for a DC filter. The detailed analysis, control schemes, simulation results, and scale-down prototype are provided to validate the effectiveness of proposed trapezoidal wave operation method.

item.title: Harmonic-Constrained Optimal Modulation for Converters Connected to a Distorted Grid
item.description: Compliance with harmonic grid standards becomes particularly challenging under nonideal operating conditions, e.g., when other converters—bedsides the one of interest—inject harmonic distortions, referred to as disturbances, at the point of common coupling (PCC). This article proposes optimized pulse patterns (OPPs) for grid-connected converters with an $LCL$ filter that ensure PCC current and voltage harmonics meet the relevant harmonic grid standards, even in the presence of such disturbances. This is achieved by formulating the OPP problem as a robust optimization problem that explicitly accounts for external harmonic distortions and incorporates explicit current and voltage harmonic constraints. The proposed framework can be applied under different levels of disturbance information: 1) full knowledge of harmonic amplitudes and phases, 2) uncertain but bounded characteristics, and 3) complete lack of knowledge during the OPP computation. Numerical results and experimental tests demonstrate that the proposed OPPs achieve significantly better compliance with harmonic grid standards compared with advanced modulation strategies, such as selective harmonic elimination and conventional OPPs.

item.title: Sub/Super-Synchronous Oscillation Analysis and Suppression of Three-Phase Electric Drive System Under Weak Grid
item.description: Under weak grid, the three-phase electric drive system is prone to cause sub/super-synchronous oscillation (SSO) due to the impedance interaction, which seriously threatens power system stability. First, the SSO mechanism and characteristics of the electric drive system under weak grid are analyzed based on impedance method. Owing to the negative resistance characteristics of the motor system, the system stability declines with the decrease of grid strength, which can cause SSO. Then, by analyzing and establishing the specific relationship between the DC-side oscillation and the AC-side oscillation, a coupling oscillation self-suppression control strategy of the rectifier is proposed. By applying the DC-side voltage fluctuation, the proposed control can directly adjust the AC-side voltage without the need of voltage and current loop control, which can improve the response speed and enhance the system's oscillation suppression capability. Compared with the existing oscillation suppression strategies, the proposed control strategy can more effectively damp SSO and improve the steady-state performance of the system. Finally, the proposed control and the theoretical analysis are verified by simulation and experimental results.

item.title: Modeling and Small-Signal Stability Analysis of Virtual Synchronous Generator Control With Dynamics of Field Winding Electromagnetic Transient and E-ω Coupling
item.description: Based on different synchronous generator models, the existing virtual synchronous generator (VSG) controls can be implemented with/without the field winding electromagnetic transient and/or E-ω coupling dynamics. However, it remains unclear which implementation provides the best small-signal stability. This article performs thorough analyses on the small-signal stability impacts of integrating the dynamics of field winding electromagnetic transient and E-ω coupling in the basic VSG control. It is revealed that a coupling between the q-axis current and the reactive power control output voltage is introduced by incorporating the field winding electromagnetic transient dynamics, which creates a potential right-half-plane pole and jeopardizes system stability. In contrast, the inclusion of the E-ω coupling dynamics introduces a phase-leading component, thereby improving system stability. Based on these findings, recommendations are provided to inform the design of advanced VSG control. The theoretical analyses are validated through experimental tests.

item.title: Topology and Control of Series-Shunt Enhanced Soft Normally Open Point Integrated With PV and ES for Medium-Voltage Direct-Linked Grid Connection
item.description: The enhanced soft normally-open point (e-SNOP) regulates the power flow of medium-voltage AC (MVAC) feeders while providing low-voltage DC (LVDC) ports for the flexible integration of distributed energy resources. However, its implementation typically requires a large number of power converters. To address this issue, this article proposes a series- shunt e-SNOP topology integrated with photovoltaic (PV) and energy storage (ES). The shunt unit of this topology offers multiple LVDC ports, enabling the direct connection of distributed PV generation and ES, whereas the series unit features expendable MVAC feeder interfaces to support flexible power flow regulation. Meanwhile, this topology adopts a single-stage DC–AC submodule (SM) design, reducing capacitors and simplifying control strategy complexity. First, this article introduces the topological configuration and its SM working principles. Then, the average circuit model is established. Additionally, the three operation modes and their corresponding control strategies are designed. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control strategies is verified through a 10-kV/3-MW full power simulation platform, and a 1-kW scaled-down experimental prototype is built to further demonstrate the correctness of the proposed topology.

item.title: An LCC-LCC Compensated Simultaneous Wireless Power and Data Transfer System With Flexible Regulation and Load-Independent Constant Voltage Output
item.description: In this article, an LCC-LCC compensated simultaneous wireless power and data transfer system is proposed to realize load-independent constant voltage (CV) output and suppress the power carrier frequency and high-frequency interference of the data transfer channel. Power is transferred via a pair of DD coils while data is directly injected into and extracted from one single D-coil, and the additional trap inductor is omitted. Combined with the characteristics of conventional LCC compensation network, a specially designed LCC compensation topology is utilized on the receiving side, both for CV output regulation and high-frequency interference suppression. The single D-coil is fully compensated by the compensation capacitors at the power carrier frequency, inhibiting the power carrier interference significantly. The performance of the power is analyzed, and the condition of the CV output regulation is provided. The mechanism of the interference suppression is explored, and the influence of the circuit parameters on the noise suppression is also discussed. It is proven that the CV output with high-quality data transfer can be obtained in the proposed system. Finally, an experimental prototype with 104.5 V output voltage and 200 kbps data rate is designed to verify the feasibility of the proposed system.

item.title: Design of an Integrated Solution for Metal-Object Detection and Interference Suppression in CPT Systems
item.description: Capacitive power transfer (CPT) technology has been widely studied in drone applications due to its advantages of lightweight structure, flexible design, and no eddy current loss. However, metal objects (MOs) can alter the coupler's electric field distribution and circuit parameters, which negatively affect system power factor, output power, and efficiency. To detect and suppress the influence of MOs in CPT system, this article proposes an integrated MO detection and MO interference suppression method using an automatic tuning assist circuit (ATAC). First, the impact of MOs on mutual capacitances, input impedance, and output current of the multicell single capacitance-coupled CPT system has been analyzed. Then, an ATAC network is designed on the primary side. By recognizing the DC voltage amplitude of the ATAC network, the impact of MOs on the CPT system can be detected when the CPT system operates in zero-voltage switching (ZVS) state. Furthermore, to suppress the interference of MOs, a constant current (CC) output method based on the secondary-side resonant frequency tracking was proposed. By recognizing the DC voltage of the ATAC network, the secondary-side resonant frequency in the LC–LC topology can be obtained. At the new operating frequency, the ATAC network adapts the circuit impedance to achieve CC output despite MO interference while maintaining a high power factor and ZVS characteristics. Finally, an experimental prototype was built, demonstrating that the output current varies within a ±6% range, and the system efficiency reaches up to 80.8%.

item.title: Wireless Power Transfer for Maritime Application: A Study on Dual-Medium Capacitive Coupler
item.description: The escalating demand for efficient maritime power transfer has driven the exploration of wireless power transfer (WPT) solutions. This article aims to develop a novel dual-medium capacitive coupler that leverages the characteristics of air and seawater to overcome limitations in traditional capacitive power transfer. Traditional capacitive couplers that use air alone exhibit low power density, while those relying solely on seawater suffer from high self-coupling due to its conductivity, which impedes effective power transfer. The proposed dual-medium coupler mitigates these issues by situating one set of plates in seawater and another in air, thus eliminating self-coupling constraints and ensuring consistent underwater coupling regardless of distance or misalignment. This innovative approach reduces space and cost requirements, providing a promising alternative for maritime power transfer. The study provides a detailed explanation of the coupler’s structure and principles, parameter design methodology, and experimental validation. Notably, the proposed coupler achieves a significant power output of 10 kW with a DC-to-DC efficiency of up to 94.8%. This underscores the potential of the novel coupler to address WPT challenges in maritime environments.

item.title: An Improved Load–Impedance–Insensitive Parallel Resonant Inverter With Stable Voltage Output and Its WPT System Application
item.description: To improve the performance of the parallel resonant inverter and expand its range of applications, an improved load–impedance–insensitive (LII) parallel resonant inverter is presented in this article, along with its MHz wireless power transfer (WPT) system application. By integrating a simple auxiliary circuit, the proposed inverter achieves high adaptability to diverse application requirements without compromising other circuit parameter designs. Based on the extended impedance method, the design fully leverages its potential by targeting partial zero-current switching operation and achieving a stable voltage output. Considering the parameter variations robustness, load reactance insensitivity is analyzed, and output power capability is incorporated into the parameter selection process. In the experiment, the proposed inverter serves as a transmitter in a WPT system, and a series–series/parallel compensation network is adopted to achieve load-independent voltage output. Finally, the prototype WPT system achieves 85% power transfer efficiency at 1 MHz, accompanied by voltage stability with only 4.97% fluctuations. Also, the proposed inverter maintains LII operation under wide impedance variations. Experimental results verify the LII operation of the proposed inverter and its remarkable adaptability.

item.title: Innovative Probabilistic Optimization of Resonance Compensation in Dynamic Wireless Power Transfer
item.description: This article presents an innovative methodology for designing resonance compensation in dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) systems for electric vehicles. The proposed technique leverages an optimization algorithm to identify the optimal combination of components that maximizes transferred energy while maintaining high transmission efficiency, achieving an effective tradeoff between these key performance metrics. Unlike conventional design approaches, this method incorporates component tolerances and employs Monte Carlo simulations to estimate the probability of meeting desired performance. This probabilistic analysis accounts for the combined effects of multiple tolerances, providing a robust evaluation of the solution’s reliability by considering both manufacturing variations and aging effects. Designers can use this probabilistic analysis to refine their choices by selecting components with stricter tolerances or exploring alternative designs. Furthermore, a MATLAB-based application, called MatDWPT, has been developed to automate the design process. This user-friendly tool enables designers to input system specifications and receive optimized component recommendations, with all circuit analysis and optimization steps performed transparently in the background. The results obtained using the toolbox align with experimental measurements. MatDWPT is open access and available on IEEE Data Port. Experimental validation demonstrates output power improvements of up to 500 W for series–series (SS) and LCC–S under misalignment conditions, and efficiency gains of up to 4% for LCC–LCC, with an additional 2% improvement across the misalignment range for LCC–S. These results highlight the proposed methodology’s effectiveness in enhancing both power transfer capability and efficiency under varying operating conditions.

item.title: Loss Analysis and Heat Optimization for a 700-mW Inductive Power Transfer System for Implanted Brain-Computer Interface
item.description: The growing power demands of implantable medical devices, particularly brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), have driven the adoption of inductive power transfer (IPT) as a reliable and efficient energy delivery method. This article presents a comprehensive methodology to identify, evaluate, and mitigate eddy current loss (ECL) in implantable BCIs. Starting from a practical case study, an ECL model is developed to decouple the influence of excitation current and isolate dominant loss mechanisms. A novel identification framework is introduced for efficient analysis, comparison, and measurement of ECL. Through a combination of simulation and experimental validation, both conduction and ECLs are optimized independently. Finally, a representative BCI system is co-optimized for coil geometry, wire selection, and phase control to minimize heat generation. This work offers a systematic solution to mitigate thermal risks in next-generation high-power implantable devices.

item.title: Floating Coil Design for Enhanced Angular Misalignment Tolerance in WPT Systems
item.description: This article presents a novel floating receiver (Rx) coil system designed to address angular misalignment challenges in static and quasi-static wireless power transfer (WPT) applications. Unlike conventional solutions that rely on multiple coils, active alignment control, or complex compensation networks, the proposed system leverages a liquid medium to achieve passive self-alignment, ensuring stable power transfer efficiency (PTE) under extreme angular misalignment conditions. By integrating the Rx coil with a low-permittivity, low-permeability liquid, the system passively adjusts its orientation to align with the transmitter (Tx) magnetic field, maintaining efficient inductive coupling. Theoretical analysis, numerical simulations, and experimental validation are conducted across various Tx–Rx configurations, including planar circular coils and Helmholtz coils. Results demonstrate that the floating Rx system maintains high PTE even at misalignment angles up to 180°, significantly outperforming conventional setups without liquid integration. The proposed approach eliminates the need for additional alignment mechanisms while improving misalignment tolerance. It is a promising solution for angularly unstable applications such as robots and autonomous underwater vehicles. This work offers a new perspective on enhancing the adaptability of WPT systems and paves the way for further exploration of liquid-integrated passive alignment techniques.

item.title: Real-Time Parameter Estimation of Wireless Power Transfer Systems With Large Receiver Tuning Tolerance
item.description: This article proposes a real-time, transmitter (Tx)-side parameter estimation method for a series–series (S-S) wireless power transfer system with large receiver tuning tolerance. A 60° phase-shifted modulation is employed to suppress the third-order harmonic voltage generated from the Tx. A unique online training algorithm is developed to determine the unknown parameters, including the coupling coefficient, equivalent load resistance, and load reactance before the rectifier. This training algorithm iteratively adjusts the parameters to minimize the error between a theoretical model and real-time measurements of the Tx's fundamental input impedance and reflected third-order harmonics. The results are used to derive the output voltage and current. A practical prototype was built and tested with varying coupling (0.4–0.7) and loads (20–80 Ω). Experimental results show accurate estimation of all parameters and variables with a time delay under 30 ms.

item.title: Development of a Simultaneous Wireless Power and Data Transfer System Based on Split Transformer
item.description: Simultaneous power and data transfer to enable fast communication between primary and secondary sides of wireless power transfer (WPT) systems is gaining attention to enhance system performance and to allow interoperability. The present work details the design, numerical simulation, and test of a simultaneous wireless power and data transfer (SWPDT) system for battery charging of autonomous mobile robotic platforms. The system implements independent multiple inductive channels for power and full-duplex data transfer, all housed in the same split transformer topology based on commercially available E-shape ferrite cores. In particular, a main winding supplied by a wide-bandgap semiconductor-based resonant converter is used to transfer power, while auxiliary data windings on the outer wings of the E-shaped core are used to obtain low-latency bidirectional data communication, implemented using the Manchester coding technique. The feasibility of the proposed SWPDT structure is verified through simulation and validated on a laboratory prototype, with a rated power of 600 W and a data rate of 10 Mbps. The experimental results are shown to be in agreement with the simulation results, achieving a system efficiency above 90% in nominal operating condition.

item.title: Analytical Model of Rectangular Coils With Planar Magnetic Cores Based on Improved Image Method for WPT Systems
item.description: The coupler is the core element of magnetically coupled wireless power transfer systems. Performing electromagnetic parameter analysis, particularly the modeling of self-inductance (SI), resistance, and mutual inductance (MI), is essential for system predesign and performance evaluation. In this article, an analytical modeling method for couplers composed of rectangular coils with planar magnetic cores is proposed based on an improved image method. The influence of core size on parameter modeling is explicitly addressed within the improved image method. First, the modeling of SI, resistance, and MI for air-core coils is established. Then, the limitations of the standard image method are discussed. To enhance its practicality, correction methods are introduced and the adjustment ranges of all potential correction terms are derived. An improved image method is then proposed to account for core effects, enabling refined analytical modeling of the SI, resistance, and MI. Results show that the proposed method achieves errors below 12% compared with finite element analysis and below 15% compared with experiments, validating its accuracy and effectiveness.

item.title: A 4.21-kW Ultrathin 1.2-mm Stackable Capacitive Coupler for High-Efficiency MHz Capacitive Power Transfer
item.description: This article presents a 1.2-mm-thick ultrathin stacked capacitive coupler (SCC) that provides a lightweight and compact isolated power conversion interface for space-constrained MHz high-power applications. Unlike conventional four-plate and six-plate capacitive couplers in capacitive power transfer systems that rely on horizontally distributed electrodes, the proposed SCC introduces a vertically stacked multilayer structure with independently insulated copper-foil plates, which suppresses nonadjacent cross-coupling effectively and significantly enhances the mutual capacitance. Its four-capacitor equivalent model is derived, and comprehensive voltage-stress and safety analyses are performed to guide plate arrangement and design at MHz frequencies. A 1-MHz SCC prototype achieves 4.21-kW peak power, 95.29% peak DC–DC efficiency, 99.87% peak AC–AC efficiency, and a power density of 83.16-kW/m2 for the active capacitive coupling area, demonstrating excellent compactness and energy transfer capability. High-frequency voltage and thermal endurance tests and FEM simulations further validate the SCC's reliability. Owing to its ultrathin profile, high-voltage endurance, simple fabrication, and high power density, the proposed SCC serves as a promising modular power supply solution for compact power modular, transportation electrification, and data center power supply.

item.title: Modeling and Analysis of Tetris-Shaped Coils for Dynamic Wireless Power Transfer Systems
item.description: Dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) can wirelessly charge devices on the move and keep the system at high working efficiency. Tetris-shaped coils are first proposed in this article and applied to DWPT systems. Due to the structural characteristics of Tetris-shaped coils, they can ensure stable and reliable connections between adjacent coils. In addition, universal models are derived and used to analyze the features of all Tetris-shaped coils. The proposed models are suitable for any polygonal coils. The mutual inductance variations of Tetris-shaped coil arrays are proved to be quite small, which means lower output power fluctuations. Finally, Tetris-shaped and square coil arrays are established to verify the model accuracy and output performance. Results showcase that the average calculation errors of self-inductances and mutual inductances are less than 3.58% and 4.05%, respectively, and the output voltage fluctuation of T-shaped coils is 14%, which is much smaller than that of square coils at 26%.

item.title: A Matrix-Type Back-to-Back Three-Phase Isolated AC–AC Converter
item.description: This article proposes a matrix-type back-to-back three-phase isolated AC–AC converter consisting of a three-phase rectifier, a dual active bridge (DAB) converter, and a three-phase inverter. Both the rectifier and inverter employ a two-phase-clamped discontinuous pulse-width modulation strategy, which is characterized by two phase modulation signals being clamped and the other one phase arm with high-frequency modulation. Moreover, for the rectifier and inverter, a control method in dq synchronous reference frame with a wide range of reactive power control capability is developed. Due to the time-varying primary-side and secondary-side six-pulse DC-link voltages, a single-phase-shift modulation strategy with variable phase shift angle is developed for the DAB to maintain constant transferred power and balanced instantaneous input and output power. In this way, symmetric and sinusoidal inputs/outputs are achieved with the cancellation of the bulky DC-link capacitors required in conventional topologies. The proposed topology integrates the advantages of traditional voltage-source type back-to-back converter and DAB converter, including sinusoidal inputs and outputs, extended reactive power control range, high power density, and high conversion efficiency. The effectiveness of the presented methods is verified by a 5-kW experimental prototype.

item.title: Design and Analysis of a Single-Phase Multilevel AC–AC Converter With Inherent Commutation
item.description: In this article, the design and analysis of a single-phase multilevel AC–AC converter with inherent commutation are proposed. This converter features a common ground configuration and ensures continuous input and output currents. It can be easily scaled to accommodate various voltage levels. Inherent commutation is achieved by integrating positive and negative switching cells, incorporating an AC link, and semiconductor devices. Accordingly, commutation issues are resolved without requiring an RC snubber circuit or a dedicated safe commutation strategy, simplifying the overall switching pattern and control complexity compared to conventional counterparts. Furthermore, the number of high-frequency switches is reduced. In the three-level implementation, only two unidirectional switches operate at high frequency, while one switch is modulated at the line frequency. Additionally, the total voltage and current stresses imposed on the switches are reduced. By eliminating the body diode of high-frequency switches from the power transfer path, the proposed topology mitigates the reverse recovery issue of the body diode, thereby facilitating the use of high-frequency MOSFETs. The comparisons of the number of components, switching device power (SDP), and efficiency confirm that the proposed converter exhibits low losses, making it an economical, compact, and lightweight solution. To validate its performance, a practical three-level prototype is fabricated, with the experimental results presented and discussed in detail.

item.title: Noncommunicative Output Regulation and Efficiency Maximization for Multiple-to-One WPT System With Unknown Receiver
item.description: The control and optimization of wireless power transfer (WPT) systems with multiple transmitters face substantial challenges in practical applications due to varying coupling network and load. This article discusses the efficiency optimization of a multiple-to-one WPT system with phase-shift modulation. Then, a novel online parameter identification method for multiple-to-one WPT systems based on the concept of voltage transient detection is proposed. By inserting a sensor inductor into the primary resonant tank and monitoring its voltage response to the rising and falling edges of the square voltage inputs, both mutual inductance and output voltage can be accurately identified without a communication link. The identification principle and sensitivity are analyzed to demonstrate robustness, and the extendibility to different primary compensation structures is discussed. Afterward, constant voltage output and efficiency optimization can be achieved via the identified results. Experiment results show an identification accuracy exceeding 97% and a combined identification and closed-loop control response time of approximately 2.2 ms. Moreover, the efficiency fluctuation is limited to within 1% over a wide range of misalignments, compared with over 15% without control.

item.title: Isolated High-Voltage-Gain Full-Range Soft-Switching DC/DC Converter Using Built-In Transformer and Coupled Inductors With Minimum Design Constraints
item.description: We propose an isolated high-voltage-gain full-range soft-switching DC/DC converter, incorporating a built-in transformer (BT) and coupled inductors (CLs), with minimal design constraints of magnetic components for achieving zero-voltage switching (ZVS) operation. A state-plane trajectory analysis is adopted to evaluate the ZVS conditions of main switches, providing precise ZVS boundaries and an exact dead-time margin. On the primary side, the proposed converter employs an interleaved interface utilizing CLs to reduce input current ripple and increase voltage gain. An active-clamping technique with the BT is also implemented to realize full-range ZVS operation with minimal constraints. On the secondary side, a voltage doubler configuration easily doubles the voltage gain. Moreover, the equivalent inductance reflected from the BT and CLs, together with the output capacitors of the main switches, enables zero-current switching (ZCS) of rectifier diodes, thereby mitigating reverse-recovery problems. A 400 W prototype, operating with input voltage of 26–34 V and output voltage of 400 V, is fabricated and evaluated to verify the effectiveness of the proposed converter.

item.title: Novel LCC-CLC Compensation Network Topology for Reducing Output Voltage Fluctuations in Dynamic Wireless Power Transfer
item.description: Dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) is an innovative solution that alleviates range anxiety and reduces the size and cost of batteries. However, it faces significant challenges, particularly fluctuations in output voltage caused by variations in mutual coupling as the vehicle moves. This article introduces a novel inductor–capacitor–capacitor (LCC)-capacitor–inductor–capacitor (CLC) compensation network that ensures a stable and smooth output voltage. The inductor in the CLC compensation network is designed as a coil similar to the receiver coil, which is coupled with the transmitter coils. The mutual coupling between the compensation coil and transmitter coils is the inverse of the coupling between the receiver and transmitter coils. The compensation coil is strategically placed at a sufficient distance from the receiver coil to avoid cross-coupling. Furthermore, the proposed DWPT system achieves higher voltage and power compared to traditional systems, as the output voltage is proportional to the sum of the mutual inductances between the compensation inductor and transmitter coils, and between the receiver and transmitter coils. Experimental results validate the proposed approach’s effectiveness in providing stable output voltage, with a fluctuation rate under $\boldsymbol{\pm}{\mathbf{4}\boldsymbol{\%}}$ as the receiver moves across the transmitter coils.

item.title: Fractional-Order Model of DAB Converter for Oscillation Analysis in High-Frequency Link Inductor Current
item.description: The dual active bridge (DAB) converter plays a significant role in emerging DC power system. However, the high frequency oscillation of inductor current in DAB converter may affect the sneak stability. Besides, previous modeling methods for DAB converters cannot precisely characterize high frequency inductor current dynamics, limited to integer-order framework, neglecting the nonlinear switching performance under fractional-order framework. Thus, a fractional-order modeling method of DAB converter is proposed for oscillation of high-frequency inductor current. First, a fractional-order model of DAB based on the generalized state-space averaging method is proposed and developed through Fourier series expansion of nonlinear switching function, to achieve an accurate high-frequency inductor current. It reveals that the fractional-order performance of DAB converter is from nonlinear switching behaviors fundamentally. Second, the high-frequency inductor current oscillation is analyzed quantitatively, revealing the impacts of controller on current oscillation. Compared with conventional DAB modeling methods, the proposed fractional-order model integrates circuit switching nonlinearities and control delay for the accurate oscillation of inductor current in DAB converter. Finally, experiment is setup to validate the inductor current oscillations and the analytical accuracy of the proposed model.

item.title: Grid Impedance Estimation During Large SCR Drop Events With Grid-Forming Converters
item.description: Grid-connected converters face significant synchronization challenges when suddenly exposed to ultraweak grid conditions, primarily due to the large and unknown grid impedance. This article proposes a passive method for efficiently estimating grid impedance during large short-circuit ratio (SCR) drop disturbances. The proposed method relies solely on local real-time measurements—specifically, voltage magnitude, active power, and reactive power at the point of common coupling (PCC)—to estimate fundamental grid impedance during transients. By leveraging the superior voltage control capability of grid-forming (GFM) converters, PCC voltage magnitude remains stable throughout the estimation process, thereby satisfying a requirement of the proposed approach. Furthermore, this method is inherently robust to grid frequency variations and phase angle jumps, further enhancing its practical applicability. With efficient impedance estimation, the converter can promptly adjust its active power reference to maintain synchronization. The effectiveness of this method has been validated through experimental results.

item.title: A Carrier-Based DPWM Method With Integrated Reactive Power Compensation Capability for Vienna Rectifier
item.description: Vienna rectifiers are widely used in many industrial applications. However, the unidirectional conduction characteristic of the diodes makes it has poor reactive power compensation capability under conventional control. In this article, the reactive power compensation capability of the Vienna rectifier is enhanced by applying a current distortion elimination method, and the corresponding feasible power factor is discussed in detail. By considering the coupling characteristics between impedance angle, modulation index, and power factor angle, the relationship between the reactive power compensation capability and the currently transmitted active current is more accurately described. Besides, a carrier-based discontinuous pulse width modulation (CB-DPWM) strategy with integrated reactive power compensation capability is proposed, the advantage of the proposed CB-DPWM is that the reference voltage has a longer clamping region with maximum absolute current, which is much helpful for reduction of switching losses. Simulation and experiments results verified the correctness of the theoretical analysis on maximum reactive power compensation capability and the superiority of the proposed CB-DPWM strategy.

item.title: A Modular Multiactive-Bridge Converter Combining Multiwinding Transformers and High-Frequency Link With Fewer Elements and Power Loss
item.description: To reduce the number of components and power loss in modular multiactive bridge (MMAB) converters used for vehicle-grid-interaction systems, this article proposes an MMAB converter that combines multiwinding transformers (MWT) and a high-frequency link (HFL), which is called MMAB-MH. The MMAB-MH converter connects multiple H-bridges of power ports to a single HFL through different MWTs, which maintains a good scalability while having fewer transformers than the conventional MMAB. This means lower core losses, copper losses, and cost. The topology of the MMAB-MH converter and its characteristics of the port expansion are described. The mathematical model is developed to characterize the power transfer among different ports. An online closed-loop control scheme for the proposed MMAB-MH converter is designed. A prototype of the four-port MMAB-MH converter is built and compared with the conventional MMAB converter to validate the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed converters. Experimental results reveal higher efficiency is achieved with fewer components.

item.title: Talkative Power Converter-Based Smart Battery Module for Intercell Balancing and Communication
item.description: As the deployment of large-scale energy storage systems for electric vehicles (EVs) and grid support grows, challenges such as cell inconsistency, reduced operational lifespan, and high maintenance costs become critical barriers. This article proposes a scalable smart battery architecture to tackle these issues. Each battery cell is paired with a smart battery cell management unit (SBCMU), and these SBCMUs are serially chain-connected. This setup provides a dedicated, independent pathway for intercell energy balancing and communication that does not interfere with the main power flow of the battery pack. Talkative power converter (TPC) technology is used in the SBCMU to convert power and facilitate communication between adjacent units. By leveraging TPC, the proposed architecture enables simultaneous intercell energy balancing and hop-by-hop data communication over the existing power path, eliminating the need for dedicated communication wiring and external transceivers. Experimental validation on a 16-cell, 48 V/100 Ah lithium-ion battery pack demonstrates that the proposed architecture effectively reduces state-of-charge deviation while maintaining robust communication during high-current operations. The results suggest that TPC-based smart battery systems can support efficient, modular, and long-life energy storage solutions.

item.title: Design and Analysis of a High-Torque-Density Brushless Hybrid Magnet Vernier Machine With Reduced Rare-Earth Material
item.description: Permanent magnet Vernier motors demonstrate high torque density characteristics, making them a promising candidate for low-speed direct drive applications. However, achieving high torque density and power factor requires excessive consumption of permanent magnets. This article investigates the topology of a Vernier motor with the combination of both rare earth and ferrite magnets, to achieve the goal of significantly reduced rare earth material. The working principle is modeled by using the combined subdomain and analytical method. Various slot/pole combinations are investigated and compared for optimal performance metrics. A comprehensive comparison is conducted between the proposed topology and conventional counterparts with rare-earth magnet. By using parallel magnetization for rare-earth and ferrite magnets, the proposed topology achieves high torque density of 32 N·m/L, high power factor of 0.87 and high torque per unit of rare-earth magnet volume of 730 N·m/L. A prototype is fabricated to verify the proposed assumption.

item.title: Nonlinear PI-Type Speed Control Design and Its Application to Monitoring of a Cooling Fan System With Perturbations
item.description: The increasing power density of modern electronic systems has intensified the need for efficient thermal management, where cooling fans play a pivotal role. However, fan performance is frequently compromised by external disturbances, aging effects, and sensor limitations. To address these challenges, this study presents a robust nonlinear proportional-integral (PI)-type speed control strategy. Unlike conventional PI or sliding mode-based designs, the proposed architecture integrates a nonlinear robustifying term within the integral path. This structural innovation filters the switching signal through the integrator and yields a continuous control input. Consequently, it effectively eliminates chattering while maintaining strong robustness against perturbations. Furthermore, a linear matrix inequality (LMI)-based design framework is established to handle system uncertainties. This systematic approach simultaneously guarantees global asymptotic stability and optimizes the control gain and convergence rate, even in the presence of bounded nonlinear aerodynamic uncertainties. Beyond robust control, the proposed method integrates physical modeling and parameter identification to enable sensorless real-time health monitoring via a digital twin. This facilitates early anomaly diagnosis without additional hardware sensors. Extensive experimental validation across various commercial cooling fan types demonstrates the robust generalizability and superior tracking accuracy of the proposed method compared to linear benchmarks. The developed framework supports real-world deployment in intelligent thermal management, fault-tolerant fan array, and condition-based maintenance applications.

item.title: Adaptive Finite Time Command Filtered Backstepping Control for Optimal Oxygen Excess Ratio of Hydrogen Fuel Cells With Unknown Air Compressor Faults
item.description: Proton exchange membrane fuel cells are a core pillar of the clean energy transition, drawing widespread attention for their high efficiency and eco-friendly energy conversion. Maintaining an optimal oxygen excess ratio (OER) is critical to balancing the efficiency, durability, and dynamic performance of PEMFC systems, but this task faces inherent nonlinearities, external disturbances, and unpredictable concurrent air compressor faults—challenges that traditional control strategies struggle to address simultaneously. Its core innovations are reflected in three aspects. First, it establishes a unified adaptive control architecture to address the performance limitations of existing command-filtered backstepping frameworks under air compressor fault conditions. Rigorous Lyapunov stability analysis verifies the closed-loop stability under parameter perturbations. Second, it develops a new fault-tolerant control paradigm to ensure the finite-time convergence of the OER under multiple concurrent unknown faults of the air compressor, without relying on external fault diagnosis modules. Third, its practical feasibility is verified through controller-hardware-in-the-loop (C-HIL) experiments. Compared with mainstream methods, the adaptive finite-time command-filtered backstepping (AFTCFB) controller exhibits better tracking accuracy, smaller overshoot, and faster fault recovery speed.

item.title: Multi-segment Pulse Charging Strategy for Prolonging the Service Life of Semi-solid-State Batteries
item.description: As a pivotal transitional technology in battery energy storage systems, semi-solid-state batteries (SSSBs) are currently undergoing an accelerated deployment phase. While pulse charging technology has been extensively investigated to enhance the charging performance of liquid lithium-ion batteries (LLIBs), its impact on SSSBs remains largely unexplored. This work presents the first comprehensive comparative analysis of pulse charging effects on both LLIBs and SSSBs, systematically evaluating critical parameters including maximum temperature rise, discharge capacity, charging speed, and cycle lifetime. Notably, SSSBs with high-impedance characteristics exhibit heightened sensitivity to charging protocols and current parameters. A novel multi-segment pulse charging (M-SPC) strategy is consequently developed. For both battery types, the M-SPC protocol demonstrates remarkable cycle lifetime extension compared to conventional constant current (CC) charging. After 1000 high-current cycles, capacity retention rates reach 84.7% for LLIBs (ten segments and 1 Hz) and 93.7% for SSSBs (ten segments and 0.004 Hz) under M-SPC operation. In contrast, SSSBs subjected to CC charging exhibit swelling-induced rupture and complete failure after merely 800 cycles. Furthermore, nonlinear correlations between pulse segment number/frequency and capacity retention are quantitatively characterized to identify the optimal parameters. These findings provide technical support for the development of electric vehicles and the accelerated application of SSSBs.

item.title: Autonomous Predefined-Time Distributed Control for Multihybrid Energy Storage Systems With State-of-Charge Balance
item.description: This article proposes an autonomous predefined-time distributed control (PDTDC) strategy for multihybrid energy storage systems in DC microgrids. The strategy enables an adjustable current sharing ratio and voltage regulation within a predefined time, while also maintaining state-of-charge (SoC) balance among energy storage units. The predefined-time disturbance observer (PDTDO) is developed to estimate mismatched disturbances, such as parameter uncertainties and load variations. Based on local and global average information, an adaptive correction factor is proposed to alleviate reliance on prior knowledge and allow the control gain to be adjusted autonomously. The proposed auxiliary controller does not depend on the current data of the adjacent units, which facilitates practical implementation. Compared with some finite-time and fixed-time approaches, the proposed controller achieves predefined-time convergence regardless of the initial conditions, with the upper bound of the convergence time determined by a predefined adjustable parameter. Finally, simulations and experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed PDTDC approach under scenarios involving sudden load switching, plug and play, and link failure.

item.title: Health Monitoring of Inductor Based on the Thermal Time Constant
item.description: Inductors are essential components in power converters, and their health monitoring is critical for preventing unscheduled downtime due to failures. This article proposes a novel inductor health monitoring method based on the thermal time constant. Results show that the inductor health can be monitored by detecting the increase in the thermal time constant. Compared to existing methods, the proposed technique achieves electrothermal decoupling, eliminating the influence of current and frequency on the results. Furthermore, it is a cost-effective and onboard method for inductor health assessment. Simulations in COMSOL and experimental tests on a PWM bridge with an inductive load were performed to verify the proposed approach. Detailed analyses were also conducted on the influences of current, frequency, ambient temperature, sensor position, and the inductor structure.

item.title: Multisensor Fusion Enhanced Evidential Space Transformation Model (ESTM) for Structural Defects Inspection of Porcelain Bushings in Power Systems
item.description: The objective of this article is to present a decision fusion method for structural defect inspection of porcelain bushings in high-voltage power systems. Conventional approaches employ heterogeneous sensors to detect surface and internal defects, often resulting in sensor fusion errors caused by conflicting evidence and the one-vote veto phenomenon. An evidential space transformation model (ESTM) is proposed to address this challenge, enabling the application of Dempster–Shafer’s combination rule with a newly defined evidence model. The proposed method effectively resolves evidence conflict and veto issues commonly encountered in Dempster–Shafer theory while addressing the limitations of incomplete evidence sets. The performance enhancement is attributed to transforming the evidence model into an Euclidean norm space, facilitating the rational distribution of conflicting evidence. We have conducted the practical experiments with porcelain bushing, which is used in the current high-voltage power systems industry. Experimental results demonstrate the success of the proposed ESTM approach that improves the inspection accuracy, mitigates the impact of erroneous individual observations, and exhibits fault-tolerant characteristics. The success of this research results contributes to the prevention of unexpected power outages, which negatively impact societal safety and the economy.

item.title: Optimized SVM Strategy for Three-Phase Single-Stage Isolated Resonant AC-DC Converter to Enhance Grid Current Quality
item.description: This article investigates the grid current distortion in the three-phase single-stage isolated resonant ac-dc converters controlled by the standard current space vector modulation (SVM). By deriving the expression of the grid current based on the SVM, it reveals there is the inherent theoretical grid current control error. This error increases with the increase of the modulation index and resonant network impedance angle ($\boldsymbol{\theta}$p) and accordingly exacerbating the grid current distortion. To address this issue, an optimized SVM strategy is proposed. The active vectors sequence is redesigned to make the vector dwell times independent of and avoid the effect of the variation of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$p. Furthermore, a vector dwell time calculation method based on the resonant current integral model is introduced to eliminate the theoretical grid current error. Detailed experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses the grid current distortion and verify its accuracy and effectiveness.

item.title: Probabilistic Wind Power Forecasting With Missing Data Tolerance: An End-to-End Nonparametric Approach
item.description: Missing data occurs due to sensor failures, communication issues, or temporary gaps in the measurement process, which may significantly reduce the performance of the wind power forecasting system. Therefore, an end-to-end nonparametric approach is proposed for probabilistic wind power forecasting (WPF) incorporating missing data imputation. The proposed method comprises both end-to-end training and online application procedures. In the end-to-end training phase, a deep learning-based nonparametric forecast model undergoes iterative processes involving imputation for missing data and model training with revised loss. In the online application phase, the trained forecast model is deployed online to provide multi-step-ahead probabilistic WPF through continuously imputating online observations. Compared with other state-of-the-art benchmarks, the advantages of the proposal include: 1) the proposed method is nonparametric, i.e., no hypotheses on distribution types are needed, and 2) the proposed end-to-end training process will automatically regulate the imputed value from the deep learning-based forecast model for higher probabilistic forecast performance, thereby mitigating the negative impact of missing observations. This approach leverages the advantages of the nonparametric method and the deep learning-based end-to-end structure. As a result, the proposed approach showcases an outstanding approximation capability for the future probability distribution of nonstationary wind power while simultaneously addressing missing values. Experiments validate that the proposed end-to-end nonparametric approach is more effective in mitigating the negative impact of data missingness on forecast performance compared to other representative two-phase methods integrated with standalone missing data imputation steps. Additionally, it outperforms its parametric end-to-end counterpart across various missing rate scenarios, especially in multi-step-ahead probabilistic forecasting.

item.title: Link-Based Distributed Co-Optimization of Power Dispatch and EV Routing in Solar Pavement-Enabled Power-Transportation Networks
item.description: Solar pavements (SPs) can offer clean electricity and accommodate road traffic, thereby enabling coupled power-transportation networks (CPTNs) from the generation side. However, SPs that are inherently dual-functional may introduce conflicts in the CPTN coordination, making the integrated management of SP performance and electric vehicle (EV) routing challenging. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a link-based approach for coordinated scheduling of SP-enabled power-transportation networks. Specifically, we establish a generalized analytical SP generation model considering the actual SP structure and dynamic shadow of vehicles to guarantee the accurate SP generation estimation. Then, we develop a novel link-based mixed user equilibrium model that employs link flows as decision variables, circumventing the complex feasible route generation required by traditional route-based models. Besides, we design a tailored endogenous flow-dependent toll to adaptively and dynamically regulate traffic flows on SP links by integrating SP generation performance and link congestion level. Under mild conditions, we prove that there exists a unique equilibrium solution. To preserve privacy between two entities, a stable and adaptive alternating direction of multipliers method (SA-ADMM) based on the regularized Barzilai-Borwein scheme is developed to efficiently solve the coordinated optimization model. Numerical studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Wind Farm Power Prediction With Wake Spatiotemporal Coupling
item.description: Power prediction for wind turbine clusters under dynamic meteorological conditions faces the challenge of wake coupling effects in time and space. Therefore, this paper proposes a wind farm power prediction method that considers the spatio-temporal dynamic coupling of wake effects.By integrating wind turbine spatial distribution and real-time meteorological data to construct a dynamic graph network, the method achieves adaptive representation of wake propagation paths. A dual-drive collaborative framework is designed, embedding physical law constraints in both the spatial and temporal dimensions to address the physical distortion issues of data-driven models under extreme operating conditions. Simultaneously, a spatio-temporal decoupled feature enhancement architecture is constructed to capture the spatial correlations of wind turbines and meteorological features across multiple time scales. Experimental results demonstrate that the dynamic graph network reduces the RMSE in wind direction sudden change scenarios by 53.85%; the physical constraint mechanism compresses the momentum residual in 48-step predictions to 0.60 × 10−2; spatial and multi-scale temporal features contribute 12.84% and 15.52% to the RMSE reduction, respectively; and multi-module collaboration optimizes the average MAPE of actual wind farm predictions to 9.37%-10.91%, representing an improvement of over 27.98% compared to the best baseline.

item.title: Coordination Control Strategy of MTDC Connected Offshore Wind Farms Based on Model Predictive Control
item.description: This paper proposes a coordination control strategy (CCS) for voltage source converter (VSC) multi-terminal direct current (MTDC) connected offshore wind farms (WFs) based on model predictive control (MPC). Firstly, to optimize active and reactive power control of WFs, an MPC-based wind turbine (WT) control strategy is designed for fatigue load suppressing and keeping WTs voltage range. Secondly, to optimize the voltage of VSCs, an MPC-based adaptive droop control (ADC) of VSCs is proposed for tracking the WFs power of WF side VSCs (WFVSCs), reducing DC network power loss or achieving power sharing of grid side VSCs (GSVSCs). To formulate MPC optimization problem, the higher-order term with respect to droop coefficient is reduced order and stability of the ADC method is analyzed analysis. Finally, the combined whole system model including multiple WFs, WFVSCs and GSVSCs is used to realize multi-objective optimization. A test system consisted of 3 WFs with 100 × 5 MW WTs, 3 WFVSCs and 2 GSVSCs was used to verify the proposed combined control scheme in MATLAB/Simulink.

item.title: A Nonlinear Drivetrain Torsional Oscillation Damper for WTGs: Robust Rejection of Large Disturbances With Balanced Noise Immunity
item.description: Drivetrain torsional oscillations in wind turbine generators (WTGs) challenge power system reliability and stability due to nonlinearities and uncertainties, especially under large disturbances, while measurement noise further degrades damper performance. This paper develops a nonlinear drivetrain torsional oscillation damper (DTOD) within an active disturbance rejection control (ADRC) framework. To adapt to its standard form, the nonlinear drivetrain dynamics of grid-connected WTG are exactly transformed into a second-order Brunovsky canonical form using differential homeomorphism coordinate transformation. DTOD parameters, including those of the extended state observer (ESO) and nonlinear state error feedback (NLSEF), are Pareto-optimally tuned through normalized multi-objective frequency-domain optimization, enhancing the robust rejection of large disturbance while balancing the trade-off with noise immunity. The fully decentralized DTOD relies only on local measurements, supporting scalable deployment across large wind farms. Simulation results under various large disturbance scenarios in grid-connected single and multiple wind farms validate the effectiveness, advantages, robustness, and scalability of the proposed nonlinear WTG DTOD.

item.title: Energy Management Strategy for Photovoltaic-Energy Storage Mobile Charging Station
item.description: This paper proposes the integration of photovoltaic-energy storage charging stations with mobile charging services (MCD)to form a photovoltaic-energy storage mobile charging station (PV-ES-MCS). The PV-ES-MCS establishes a charging service framework that simultaneously achieves low-carbon environmental benefits and operational flexibility. Furthermore, an energy management strategy is developed that considers both operational economics and mobile charging scheduling requirements. Through detailed analysis of the PV-ES-MCS system architecture, comprehensive modeling of operational costs and revenues is conducted. By incorporating the energy replenishment demands of mobile charging scheduling and operational cost reduction, an energy management objective function with corresponding constraints is formulated and solved using the Grey Wolf Optimizer algorithm. Case study results demonstrate that the proposed strategy enables coordinated multi-element regulation within the PV-ES-MCS station, reducing operational costs by 6.7% while effectively ensuring the energy replenishment efficiency of mobile charging scheduling with 100% MCD battery swap success.

item.title: Dual-Time-Scale Dynamic Active Wake Control Using Stochastic Yaw Optimization and Distributed Axial Induction Optimization for Large-Scale Wind Farm
item.description: As the scale of wind farms (WFs) and the number of wind turbines (WTs) continue to increase, the calculation efficiency of centralized active wake control (AWC) methods can not satisfy the real-time performance requirement. To address this issue, a dual-time-scale dynamic AWC strategy for large-scale WF is proposed in this paper. First, a centralized AWC model is developed which incorporates a dual-time-scale control strategy. The optimal yaw angle is obtained by long-term scale stochastic optimization under the time-varying wind conditions to maximize the power generation potential of WF, and different WF operating modes can be achieved by short-term scale dynamic axial induction control. Then, a spectral clustering-based WF partitioning method is introduced. The wake coupling can be obtained by engineering wake model, and the WF can be divided into multiple subregions through efficient matrix computations and eigenvalue analysis. Finally, in order to improve the calculation speed, the centralized AWC optimization problem is decomposed into several smaller subproblems that can be solved in a distributed manner based on the partitioning results. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method obtains reasonable WF partitioning, owns excellent power generation and fatigue load suppression performance under time-varying wind conditions, and significantly improves calculation speed.

item.title: Coordination of Power and Transportation Networks With Integrated Electricity-Hydrogen Stations: A Reinforcement Learning Approach Considering Delayed Reward
item.description: The increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) and fuel cell EVs (FCEVs) has intensified the coupling between urban transportation and energy systems. However, existing approaches lack effective optimization strategies for integrated transportation–energy systems, particularly in managing integrated electricity-hydrogen stations (IEHSs) to maximize revenue while serving various vehicle types, such as EVs and FCEVs. The present work proposes a dynamic optimization strategy for IEHSs that enhances the operation of IEHSs, power distribution networks, and transportation networks by guiding vehicle charging and fueling decisions. The sensitivity of vehicular traffic to system-level decisions is ensured by establishing a mixed dynamic user equilibrium model incorporating EVs, FCEVs, and conventional-fuel vehicles. An IEHS operation framework that integrates discount coupon allocation and hydrogen production/storage strategies is designed with traffic flow feedback and distribution locational marginal pricing. The inherent delayed reward problem in transportation, where the impacts of pricing decisions on station revenue and traffic congestion manifest over extended time horizons, is addressed by applying a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning approach, featuring a dual-critic architecture based on long short-term memory networks under the sequence markov decision process framework, to redistribute delayed rewards. The results of numerical computations involving real-world traffic and power networks demonstrate that the proposed approach improves IEHS profitability, balances charging demand, and alleviates traffic congestion compared with static and semi-dynamic traffic approaches.

item.title: Multimodal Ensemble Photovoltaic Power Forecasting Incorporating Diffusion-Based Satellite Image Prediction
